LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT MARCIA SITCOSKE, ANTHONY KOPP NOTICE OF MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A CROSS COMPLAINT. NO APPEARANCE WAS MADE BY ANY PARTY, COUNSEL OR INTERESTED PERSON. THE COURT ADOPTS THE FOLLOWING TENTATIVE RULING: DEFENDANT MARCIA SITCOSKE AND ANTHONY KOPP'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A CROSS COMPLAINT IS GRANTED. DEFENDANTS' COMPULSORY CLAIMS AGAINST PLAINTIFF ARE BASED ON THE SAME TRANSACTIONS AND OCCURRENCES AS THE COMPLAINT FILED BY PLAINTIFF -- NAMELY, PLAINTIFF'S RIGHT TO ENTER INTO DEFENDANTS' UNIT AND PERFORM CONSTRUCTION THERE. (SEE CCP 426.10(C) & 426.30(A).) UNDER CCP 426.50, A DEFENDANT MAY SEEK LEAVE TO FILE A COMPULSORY CROSS-COMPLAINT THAT WAS NOT FILED AT THE TIME OF THE ANSWER "WHETHER THROUGH OVERSIGHT, INADVERTENCE, MISTAKE, NEGLECT, OR OTHER CAUSE." THE COURT MUST GRANT LEAVE TO FILE SUCH A CROSS-COMPLAINT "IF THE PARTY WHO FAILED TO PLEAD THE CAUSE ACTED IN GOOD FAITH." ID. SECTION 426.50 IS TO BE "LIBERALLY CONSTRUED TO AVOID FORFEITURE OF CAUSES OF ACTION." PLAINTIFF OFFERS NO OPPOSITION ON THIS POINT. COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE TENTATIVE RULING AND IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: RICHARD B, ULMER JR., CLERK: R. WOODS, NOT REPORTED. (302/RBU)

DEFENDANT MARCIA SITCOSKE, ANTHONY KOPP NOTICE OF MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-21-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

DEFENDANT MARCIA SITCOSKE and ANTHONY KOPP's NOTICE OF MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A CROSS COMPLAINT

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The court adopts the following tentative ruling:

DEFENDANT MARCIA SITCOSKE and ANTHONY KOPP's MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A CROSS COMPLAINT is granted. Defendants' compulsory claims against Plaintiff are based on the same transactions and occurrences as the Complaint filed by Plaintiff -- namely, Plaintiff's right to enter into Defendants' unit and perform construction there. (See CCP 426.10(c) & 426.30(a).) Under CCP 426.50, a defendant may seek leave to file a compulsory cross-complaint that was not filed at the time of the answer "whether through oversight, inadvertence, mistake, neglect, or other cause." The Court must grant leave to file such a cross-complaint "if the party who failed to plead the cause acted in good faith." Id. Section 426.50 is to be "liberally construed to avoid forfeiture of causes of action." Plaintiff offers no opposition on this point.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B, Ulmer Jr., Clerk: R. Woods, Not Reported. (302/RBU)

MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION AGAINST DEFENDANTS MARCIA SITCOSKE AND ANTHONY KOPP

No appearances. This matter is transferred to Department 302 to be heard on March 20, 2023 at 9:30 a.m. per the order of the presiding judge. Judge: Charles F. Haines, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (501/CFH)

PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR PRELMINARY INJUNCTION
Off Calendar Mar-20-2023 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR PRELMINARY INJUNCTION

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for a preliminary injunction is denied.

Preliminary injunctions are termed "extraordinary" for good reason - they are not to be granted lightly. (See 8 Witkin, California Procedure (5th ed. 2008) p. 903; Tahoe Keys Property Owners' Assn. v. State Water Resources Control Bd. (1994) 23 Cal.App.4th 1459, 1471.) In particular, when a preliminary injunction is sought, the case has yet to be adjudicated, for "no trial on the merits" has been held. (Id.; SB Liberty, LLC v. Isla Verde Assn., Inc. (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 272, 280.)

Plaintiff's preliminary injunction motion is denied for each of three initial reasons.

First, plaintiff delayed in seeking injunctive relief. (O'Connell v. Sup. Ct. (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 1452, 1481.) Plaintiff began planning his attic renovation five years ago, in 2018 (Pottebaum Dec. 2:9); sued defendants on August 25, 2022 (seven months ago) and concedes he knew by October 24, 2022 (five months ago) that defendants "would block access." (Rply. 5:14.)

Second, what plaintiff seeks is mandatory, not prohibitive, injunctive relief. He would have the court mandate that defendants immediately open their living space's doors to become a seven-nine-month construction zone with a new shear wall built within the space - all without trial. (Memo. 5:6-7; Lynch Dec. 2:15-19.) "The granting of a mandatory injunction pending trial is not permitted except in extreme cases where the right thereto is clearly established." (Teachers Ins. & Annuity Assn. v. Furlotti (1999) 70 Cal.App.4th 1487, 1493.) Such is not the case here.

Third, as plaintiff recites, injunctions "are equitable in nature" and their purpose "is to preserve the status quo pending a determination on the merits." (Memo. 7:18-19.) Plaintiff's proposed injunction would upset the status quo when trial on the merits is set for less than seven months from now, on October 9. (Id. at 6:18.)

In addition, two "interrelated factors" are typically weighed "when deciding whether to grant a plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction: (1) the likelihood that the plaintiff will prevail on the merits at trial, and (2) the relative interim harm to the parties from the issuance or nonissuance of the injunction." (SB Liberty, 217 Cal.App.4th at 280.)

At present, it appears plaintiff is unlikely to prevail on the merits at trial. The case focuses on a passage in the HOA declarations: "The Owner of Unit 734 [plaintiff] has the right, without further approval of the Owner of Unit 736 [defendants] to make the attic of Unit 734 into habitable dwelling space" (Pottebaum Dec. Ex. 1 sec. 6.6.) It is difficult to read into that passage turning defendants' dwelling space into a seven-nine-month construction zone and building a shear wall within it.

The balance of interim harms until trial also weighs in defendants' favor. Their harm - many months of construction, the wall - would be effectively irreversible. On the other hand, plaintiff argues, with little support, that delaying construction pending an October 9 trial would "disturb" his "contractual relationships." (Rply. 5:19.)

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order to the Court.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
